pattern: Every second spent before the target buyer finishes is really just someone else's single purchase, so counting how many purchases everyone else squeezes in before the target runs out collapses a whole queue simulation into a sum.
hints: You don't need to actually rotate anyone through a line -- think about how many total purchases happen, across everyone, before the target person's own last purchase.
A person ahead of the target in line gets to buy once for every round the target still has left, capped by how many tickets that person itself wants.

A person behind the target in line only gets to buy during a round the target has *already* started, so they get one fewer guaranteed round than someone ahead -- cap their contribution at the target's ticket count minus one instead.

edge_inputs.description: target sits at the very back of a three-person line
target sits at the very front and outlasts everyone
the person ahead of the target wants far more tickets but cannot slow the target beyond the target's own count
everyone wants exactly the target's ticket count, a three-way tie
target at the back behind an uneven front, so every earlier purchase still counts fully
target in the middle of a line where everyone needs exactly one ticket
target near the front with a much larger, slower neighbor right behind

stress.description: random ticket counts and a random target position, sized toward the queue-length cap
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, 100)
    tickets = [rng.randint(1, 100) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
    return [tickets, k]
